questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 10 weeks gestation presents with heavy vaginal bleeding and clots for 2 days, associated with lower abdominal cramps. On examination, the cervical os is open and there is moderate bleeding. The uterus is consistent with 8 weeks size. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The open cervical os and retained products (indicated by uterine size smaller than dates) confirm incomplete miscarriage. Complete miscarriage would have a closed os and smaller uterus. Missed miscarriage has a closed os and no bleeding. Ectopic pregnancy typically presents with unilateral pain and minimal bleeding. Threatened miscarriage has a closed os and viable pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman at 9 weeks gestation presents with mild intermittent vaginal spotting for 10 days and lower abdominal discomfort. On speculum examination, the cervical os is open but only scant blood is seen. Ultrasound shows an irregular intrauterine gestation with echogenic material and a closed internal os on transvaginal scan. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Ultrasound findings of retained echogenic material (retained products) despite closed internal os indicate incomplete miscarriage; the open external os on speculum confirms ongoing passage. Complete miscarriage would show empty cavity. Missed miscarriage has a closed os and no passage of tissue. Ectopic has empty uterus. Threatened miscarriage has a viable pregnancy and closed os.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation presents with heavy vaginal bleeding, severe lower abdominal pain, and signs of hypovolaemic shock. She has passed large clots. On examination, the cervical os is open and products of conception are visible at the os. Her pulse is 110 bpm and BP 85/50 mmHg. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Open os with visible products, heavy bleeding, and shock indicate incomplete miscarriage complicated by haemorrhage. Complete miscarriage would have settled bleeding and closed os. Septic abortion presents with fever and foul discharge. Ectopic pregnancy rarely causes such dramatic bleeding. Molar pregnancy may cause heavy bleeding but os is often closed and ultrasound shows vesicular pattern.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman at 8 weeks gestation presents with fever, lower abdominal pain, and offensive vaginal discharge. She had a surgical evacuation for miscarriage 5 days ago. On examination, temperature 38.5°C, pulse 100 bpm, cervical os is open, and there is tender uterus. She is passing small clots. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Fever, offensive discharge, recent evacuation, and open os with retained products point to septic incomplete miscarriage. Endometritis can occur after delivery but less likely with open os and retained tissue. PID typically not related to recent pregnancy. Complete miscarriage would have closed os and no fever. Ectopic unlikely with recent evacuation.

questions.stem: A 41-year-old woman at 6 weeks gestation presents with light vaginal bleeding for 5 days and mild suprapubic pain. She has had two previous miscarriages. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus with a thickened endometrial echo (15mm) and a positive urine pregnancy test. The cervical os is open on speculum. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Open os with retained tissue (thickened endometrium) and positive hCG confirm incomplete miscarriage despite minimal bleeding. Complete miscarriage would have empty cavity and negative hCG. Missed miscarriage would have closed os. Cervical ectopic shows gestational sac in cervix. Gestational trophoblastic disease has very high hCG and vesicular pattern.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 10 weeks gestation presents with vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal pain. On speculum examination, the cervical os is open and no products are visible. What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis of incomplete miscarriage?
questions.explanation: Transvaginal ultrasound is the gold standard to confirm retained products of conception. An open os without visible tissue requires imaging to assess the uterine cavity. Serum β-hCG may be useful but does not confirm the presence of retained tissue.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with heavy vaginal bleeding and passage of clots at 9 weeks gestation. She is haemodynamically stable. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterine cavity with a thin endometrium. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: An empty uterus with a thin endometrium indicates complete expulsion of pregnancy tissue. Retained products would appear as a heterogeneous intrauterine mass.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with a history of recurrent miscarriage presents with mild vaginal bleeding at 8 weeks. Transvaginal ultrasound shows no intrauterine pregnancy and a thickened endometrium. Serum β-hCG is 800 IU/L. Two days later, it is 750 IU/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A plateauing or slowly falling β-hCG with a thickened endometrium suggests retained trophoblastic tissue (incomplete miscarriage). Complete miscarriage would show a rapid fall to negative, while ectopic pregnancy often has a slower rise or plateau but typically no intrauterine pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with vaginal bleeding after a medical termination of pregnancy at 7 weeks. She has mild pain and a closed cervical os. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm whether the abortion is incomplete?
questions.explanation: Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line investigation to assess for retained products of conception. Serum β-hCG can be supportive but a single level cannot confirm tissue retention, and the closed os does not rule out incomplete miscarriage.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman at 11 weeks gestation presents with heavy vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal pain. On examination, she is tachycardic and hypotensive. Speculum shows an open os with active bleeding. What is the most appropriate investigation to diagnose incomplete miscarriage in this unstable patient?
questions.explanation: Despite haemodynamic instability, transvaginal ultrasound remains the most appropriate diagnostic test to confirm retained products and guide management if the patient can be stabilised. Transabdominal ultrasound may be used if transvaginal access is limited, but TVUS provides superior visualisation.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with a confirmed incomplete miscarriage at 10 weeks' gestation presents with heavy vaginal bleeding and products of conception visible at the cervical os. She is haemodynamically stable (pulse 90 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg). What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Surgical evacuation (suction evacuation or manual vacuum aspiration)
Oral tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily
Medical management with vaginal misoprostol 800 micrograms
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend surgical evacuation as first-line for incomplete miscarriage with heavy bleeding or when products are visible at the os, as it provides immediate haemostasis. Expectant or medical management are alternatives for less active bleeding.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with a confirmed incomplete miscarriage at 9 weeks' gestation has minimal vaginal bleeding and a small amount of retained tissue on ultrasound (16 mm). She is pain-free and haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Medical management with mifepristone 200 mg followed by vaginal misoprostol 800 micrograms
Surgical evacuation (suction evacuation or manual vacuum aspiration)
questions.explanation: NICE recommends expectant management as first-line for incomplete miscarriage with minimal bleeding and small retained tissue (≤20 mm), as it avoids interventions. Medical management is an alternative if expectant fails or is declined, but mifepristone is not routinely used in incomplete miscarriage.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with an incomplete miscarriage at 12 weeks' gestation presents with heavy bleeding, tachycardia (120 bpm), and hypotension (85/50 mmHg). Resuscitation with IV fluids is commenced. What is the single most appropriate definitive therapy?
options: Urgent surgical evacuation (suction evacuation or manual vacuum aspiration)
Blood transfusion with cross-matched red cells
IV oxytocin 5 units bolus followed by infusion
questions.explanation: In haemodynamically unstable patients with incomplete miscarriage, urgent surgical evacuation is the definitive treatment to control bleeding. While oxytocin and tranexamic acid may be used adjunctively, they do not remove the retained products of conception.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with an incomplete miscarriage at 8 weeks' gestation develops fever (38.5°C), lower abdominal tenderness, and offensive vaginal discharge. What treatment is contraindicated in this setting?
options: Surgical evacuation (suction evacuation or manual vacuum aspiration)
questions.explanation: In septic incomplete miscarriage, medical management with misoprostol is contraindicated due to the risk of worsening infection. Urgent surgical evacuation after starting antibiotics is the standard of care, as per NICE guidance.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old rhesus-negative woman with a confirmed incomplete miscarriage at 11 weeks' gestation is scheduled for surgical evacuation. Which of the following should be administered as part of her routine care?
options: Folic acid 5 mg daily for 3 months
questions.explanation: NICE recommends Anti-D immunoglobulin for all rhesus-negative women undergoing surgical evacuation for miscarriage after 12 weeks (or if instrumentation, but also for miscarriages >12 weeks; however, it is commonly given for any uterine instrumentation after the first trimester. For miscarriages under 12 weeks, Anti-D is not routinely recommended unless the uterus is instrumented, but many UK trusts still administer it for surgical evacuation. The correct answer is Anti-D immunoglobulin to prevent Rh sensitisation.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 10 weeks gestation presents to the Emergency Department with light vaginal bleeding and passage of small clots. She is haemodynamically stable. On speculum examination, the cervical os is open and products of conception are visible. There is no sign of infection. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to gynaecology for surgical evacuation
Offer expectant management with safety-netting advice
Start oral misoprostol for medical management
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend expectant management as first-line for incomplete miscarriage in a stable patient without signs of infection or heavy bleeding. Expectant management avoids unnecessary interventions and has a high success rate. The most tempting distractor is ultrasound, but it is not routinely required if the clinical diagnosis is clear.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation presents with moderate vaginal bleeding and cramping abdominal pain. She is haemodynamically stable, Hb 110 g/L. On examination, the cervical os is open and retained products are seen. She has no signs of infection and prefers to avoid surgery. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Expectant management with follow-up in 7–14 days
Urgent suction evacuation under general anaesthetic
Arrange transvaginal ultrasound to measure retained tissue
questions.explanation: For a stable patient with incomplete miscarriage who prefers medical management, NICE recommends a single dose of oral misoprostol 600 mcg. This is more appropriate than expectant management given her preference and moderate bleeding. Surgical evacuation is reserved for those who decline or fail medical management, or have heavy bleeding. Ultrasound is not mandatory as the diagnosis is clinical.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman at 14 weeks gestation presents to the Emergency Department with torrential vaginal bleeding, severe lower abdominal pain, and dizziness. She is pale and diaphoretic; BP 80/50 mmHg, HR 120 bpm. On examination, the cervical os is open with visible products of conception. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start intravenous fluids and cross-match blood
Arrange emergency suction evacuation under general anaesthesia
Perform bimanual examination to assess uterine size
questions.explanation: The patient is haemodynamically unstable. Immediate resuscitation with IV fluids and blood products is the priority before any surgical intervention. Suction evacuation is indicated but only after initial stabilisation. Ergometrine may worsen hypertension and is not first-line. Ultrasound and bimanual examination would delay resuscitation.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is undergoing manual vacuum aspiration (MVA) under local anaesthetic for an incomplete miscarriage. During the procedure, she suddenly complains of severe pain and a sensation of 'something giving way'. She becomes bradycardic (HR 45 bpm) and hypotensive (BP 80/50 mmHg). What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Stop the procedure and reassess the patient
Call the anaesthetist for urgent assistance
Insert a urinary catheter to monitor output
Perform a chest X-ray to rule out pneumothorax
questions.explanation: The first action when a complication occurs during MVA is to stop the procedure and assess the patient. This allows for identification of vasovagal reaction, uterine perforation, or haemorrhage. Atropine can be given if bradycardia persists, but stopping the procedure is the immediate priority. Calling for help may follow but is not the very first step.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman with known von Willebrand disease presents at 8 weeks gestation with vaginal bleeding. Transvaginal ultrasound confirms an incomplete miscarriage with retained products. She is haemodynamically stable, Hb 120 g/L, and has mild bleeding. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange surgical evacuation with consultant haematology input
Refer to a specialist haemophilia centre for advice only
Administer oral misoprostol for medical management
questions.explanation: In patients with bleeding disorders (e.g. von Willebrand disease), NICE recommends surgical evacuation with haematology input to manage bleeding risk. Expectant or medical management may lead to prolonged or severe haemorrhage. Tranexamic acid may be used adjunctively but is not the definitive management. Referral alone without a plan delays treatment.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with vaginal bleeding and passage of clots for 2 days. She is haemodynamically stable but has a temperature of 38.5°C. Abdominal examination reveals suprapubic tenderness. Bimanual examination shows an open cervical os and a tender, slightly enlarged uterus. Which single finding most strongly indicates that this is an emergency requiring urgent intervention?
questions.explanation: Fever in the presence of a tender uterus suggests septic miscarriage, which is a medical emergency. While heavy bleeding and open os are typical, they do not necessarily require immediate intervention if the patient is stable. Mild tachycardia may be due to pain or blood loss but is not as critical as a sign of infection.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with a confirmed incomplete miscarriage at 8 weeks is managed expectantly. She returns 3 days later with increased bleeding and cramping. On examination, she looks pale, pulse 120 bpm, BP 85/55 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 97% on air. There is a moderate amount of blood in the vagina, and the cervical os is open. Which single finding most clearly indicates that immediate surgical evacuation is required?
options: Moderate blood loss on speculum exam
questions.explanation: Hypotension (SBP <90) is a threshold for haemodynamic instability, indicating severe hypovolaemia that requires immediate IV fluids and surgical evacuation. Tachycardia is also a sign but does not alone define instability. Open os and moderate blood loss are expected; pallor is subjective.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after collapsing at home. She had been bleeding heavily for 2 hours. On arrival, she is unconscious, BP 60/40 mmHg, pulse 140 bpm, and there is profuse vaginal bleeding. Which single finding on speculum examination would confirm the diagnosis of incomplete miscarriage as the cause of the haemorrhage?
options: Visible products of conception at the os
Active bleeding from the cervical os
questions.explanation: Seeing products of conception at the cervical os confirms the diagnosis of incomplete miscarriage, allowing immediate manual removal or curettage to control bleeding. Active bleeding is non-specific; lacerations may occur but are less likely as primary cause in a patient with known pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman presents with 6 weeks amenorrhoea, vaginal spotting, and mild lower abdominal pain. Ultrasound shows an empty uterus and a right adnexal mass. Serum beta-hCG is 1500 IU/L. Which single clinical finding most strongly suggests an ectopic pregnancy rather than an incomplete miscarriage?
questions.explanation: Cervical motion tenderness is a classic sign of ectopic pregnancy due to peritoneal irritation. In incomplete miscarriage, the cervical os is typically open and bleeding may be heavy, but these are not specific to ectopic. Ultrasound already shows empty uterus, so ectopic is suspected; cervical motion tenderness supports it.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman underwent surgical evacuation for an incomplete miscarriage at 12 weeks. She returns 4 weeks later reporting persistent nausea and vaginal bleeding. Serum beta-hCG is 25,000 IU/L. Which single finding most strongly suggests she has gestational trophoblastic disease rather than retained products of conception?
questions.explanation: A beta-hCG level that remains markedly elevated (>20,000 IU/L) at 4 weeks post-evacuation is highly suspicious for gestational trophoblastic disease. While uterine size and theca lutein cysts can occur, the key red flag is the persistently high hCG. Nausea and bleeding are non-specific.
